coredata.dc:title: Emission enhanced fluorometric biosensor by functionalized carbon polymer dots for glutathione detection in human real samples and molecular logic gate operation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xi
Hu, Yun
Xu, Lina
Li, Yufei
Zhang, Lifang
Cao, Yufeng
Zhou, Jinqiu
Qian, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH), an active peptide, plays pivotal roles in many physiological processes and detection of GSH inside of human body is of great importance for the playing of its biological effects. Here silver-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots (Ag@PCPDs) were prepared via solvothermal treatment of citric acid and phytic acid in the presence of Ag+ for GSH determination. The physicochemical and optical performance of the Ag@PCPDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy analyses. The prepared Ag@PCPDs have outstanding water solubility with high monodispersity (7.81 ± 0.31 nm) and exhibited excellent optical properties with excitation-dependent emission, high photostability, pH, and ionic strength tolerance. An optimized excitation at 358 nm, the Ag@PCPDs showed strong photoluminescent (PL) emission at 456 nm with a PL quantum yield (QYs) of 15.6%. Furthermore, the Ag@PCPDs were used as a PL sensing platform for detection GSH in a linear range of 0–200 μM with a low limit of detection at 0.68 μM. In addition, the proposed system can construct molecular logic gates with GSH and Fe3+ ions as the chemical inputs and PL emissions as the output. And the Ag@PCPDs were successfully used for GSH determination in real samples resulting in high sensitivity and satisfactory recoveries (92.81––107.45%). More importantly, the Ag@PCPDs showed low cytotoxicity at 500 μg/mL and superior cell imaging capability in HeLa cells, which offer a new path for detection and categorization of GSH in biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Photoluminescent
Glutathione
Real sample
Logic gates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL detection of GSH by Ag@PCPDs
Detection of GSH in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32001284 , 318907733 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (HR) image of Ag@PCPDs. (b) The size distribution of Ag@PCPDs. (c) The FT-IR spectra of citric acid, phytic acid and Ag@PCPDs. (d) The XPS spectrum of Ag@PCPDs.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) P 2p and (d) Ag 3d in Ag@PCPDs.

(a) The UV–visible absorption and PL spectra of Ag@PCPDs. (inset: photographs taken under 365 nm UV light and daylight) (b) PL intensities of Ag@PCPDs versus emission wavelengths and excitation wavelengths. The influence of (c) pH and (d) different ionic strengths (K+, Ca2+ and Na+) on the PL intensities of Ag@PCPDs.

(a) The PL intensity of the Ag@PCPDs against GSH concentrations in a range of 0–200 μM. The inset is the corresponding linear plot in the GSH concentration range of 0–200 μM. (b) Specificity of Ag@PCPDs probe toward GSH and representative interferent, including Glu, DA, CA, Dopac, AA, Cys and UA.
The truth table for molecular logic gate and operational working of output logic gate.
The Cell viability in the presence of different concentrations of the Ag@PCPDs.
The fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with Ag@PCPDs, (left) he fluorescent images, (middle) the bright-field images, (right) the merged images of both.

head.ce:title: Emission enhanced fluorometric biosensor by functionalized carbon polymer dots for glutathione detection in human real samples and molecular logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Nanjing 210042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Nanjing 210042, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pinghu Institute of Advanced Materials, Zhejiang University of Technology, Pinghu 314200, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pinghu Institute of Advanced Materials, Zhejiang University of Technology, Pinghu 314200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme showing the PL responses of the Ag@PCPDs towards different concentrations of GSH and operational working of output logic gate.

Glutathione (GSH), an active peptide, plays pivotal roles in many physiological processes and detection of GSH inside of human body is of great importance for the playing of its biological effects. Here silver-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots (Ag@PCPDs) were prepared via solvothermal treatment of citric acid and phytic acid in the presence of Ag+ for GSH determination. The physicochemical and optical performance of the Ag@PCPDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy analyses. The prepared Ag@PCPDs have outstanding water solubility with high monodispersity (7.81 ± 0.31 nm) and exhibited excellent optical properties with excitation-dependent emission, high photostability, pH, and ionic strength tolerance. An optimized excitation at 358 nm, the Ag@PCPDs showed strong photoluminescent (PL) emission at 456 nm with a PL quantum yield (QYs) of 15.6%. Furthermore, the Ag@PCPDs were used as a PL sensing platform for detection GSH in a linear range of 0–200 μM with a low limit of detection at 0.68 μM. In addition, the proposed system can construct molecular logic gates with GSH and Fe3+ ions as the chemical inputs and PL emissions as the output. And the Ag@PCPDs were successfully used for GSH determination in real samples resulting in high sensitivity and satisfactory recoveries (92.81––107.45%). More importantly, the Ag@PCPDs showed low cytotoxicity at 500 μg/mL and superior cell imaging capability in HeLa cells, which offer a new path for detection and categorization of GSH in biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a kind of cellular metabolic substances, which has attracted great attention in medicine, cosmetics and food processing industry due to its remarkable biological function, such as signal transduction, redox homeostasis and chelation of metal ions [1]. For example, GSH was reported to be able to activate antioxidant enzymes and scavenge free radicals, showing significant prophylaxis effect on cardiovascular disease, scurvy, acquired immune deficiency syndrome, spiritual illnesses and chemical-induced canceration [2]. Furthermore, GSH serves as an intracellular thiol, which plays key roles in keeping cellular components off being injury by toxins and reactive oxygen species (ROS) [3]. The abnormal level of the intracellular thiol may lead to numerous clinical diseases [4]. Thus, a rapid, selective and sensitive detection of GSH inside of human body is crucial for the playing of its biological effects. A series of analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], capillary electrophoresis [6], electrochemistry [7], and chemiluminescence [8], have been developed for GSH determination in various samples. However, time-consuming preparation, complicated operations and sophisticated equipment are still needed by the aforementioned methods, restricting the application in the field of fast monitor GSH [9]. Therefore, the strategies for rapid and effective GSH determination are still a challenge.

Fluorescent probes techniques have been considered as one of the most promising analytical approaches towards GSH, due to their advantages of technical simplicity, prompt response, high specificity and sensitivity [10–12]. Fluorescent carbon polymer dots (CPDs) have attracted increasing attention in various areas, such as bio-labeling, cell-imaging, drug-delivery and environmental monitoring [13–15]. And the fluorescent probes based CPDs also can be regarded as a versatile platform for fluorescence sensing and intracellular imaging because of its unique photostability and fluorescence emission, excellent water solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [16]. However, the low quantum yields (QYs) and few functional surface groups of the pristine CPDs have limited their applications based on their optical performances. Chemical heteroatoms doping, one of the widely used methods to regulate the photoluminescent properties, aims to change the energy gaps in the electronic structures of CPDs by introducing non-metal atomic or metal ions into CPDs, endowing the CPDs with different intrinsic properties [17]. Sun et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots fluorescence probe for GSH detection [10]. Doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms is helpful to improve the intrinsic optical performances of carbon dots. Huang’s group reported a Cu2+-mediated CDs for GSH sensing [3]. The introducing of Cu ions enhanced the sensitivity towards GSH. However, the strategies based on the non-metal atomic and metal ions co-doping CPDs for the detection of GSH are rarely reported. And it is valuable to utilize the synergistic effect of non-metal atomic and metal ions to further construct GSH biosensors.

In this work, silver-phosphorus co-doped CPDs (Ag@PCPDs) were synthesized in the presence of Ag+ through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and phytic acid. The doped P enabled to adjust electron density distribution and energy gap of CPDs, inducing a significant improvement of photoluminescent (PL) performance. The introducing of Ag+ on the surface of CPDs facilitated fluorescence recognition behaviors towards GSH. And the synergistic effect between P and Ag+ offers high sensitivity and selectivity of CPDs towards GSH and the PL linear responses of Ag@PCPDs to GSH were found in a concentration range of 0–200 μM with a low limit of detection down of 0.68 μM. And the proposed PL sensing platform towards GSH was applied in real serum and urine samples and good recoveries (92.81%∼107.45%) were obtained by standard addition method. Furthermore, the intracellular assays displayed that Ag@PCPDs had excellent biocompatibility that can satisfy the prerequisite for the application in real sample determination. Benefit from the above unique characteristics, a sophisticated logic system based on Ag@PCPDs was proposed, which suggested that the as-fabricated PL aptasensor based Ag@PCPDs could effectively and rapidly monitor GSH in various complicated domains.

A variety of metal salts including AgNO3, AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, Co(NO3)2, CuCl2, FeCl3, KOH, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), dopamine (DA), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (Dopac), phytic acid (PA), citric acid (CA), glutathione (GSH) and uric acid (UA) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were made using deionized water.

CA (0.5 g), AgNO3 (0.25 g) and PA (0.25 g) were dissolved into 30 mL deionized water, which was then treated by ultrasound for 30 min. After that, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated to 160 ℃ for 6 h, further dialyzed by dialysis bag (1000 Da cut off) for 12 h. The resultant powder was acquired by freeze-drying for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL detection of GSH by Ag@PCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: A dispersion of the Ag@PCPDs was prepared in PBS buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.0) with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. 300 μL of Ag@PCPDs dispersion, 1200 μL of GSH solution with different concentration (0–300 μM) were mixed in a quartz cuvette. And then the solution was diluted to the mark with deionized water followed by being incubated at room temperature for 30 min. For selectivity experiment, a series of interfering species were studied by adding one of them (300 μM) into Ag@PCPDs system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The potential application of the proposed bioassay was demonstrated by applying it to detecting GSH in urine and serum samples [10]. A quantity of the GSH was added to a 100-fold diluted serum and urine samples before fluorescent analysis and no other pretreatments were necessary [18]. The content of the GSH was determined by the standard addition method [19].

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (UV-1800pc) at 800–200 nm with Ag@PCPDs concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were collected at 200 kV in a JEM-2200FS (JEOL, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were obtained on iS10 FT-IR spectrometer at 500–5000 cm−1 (Nicolet, USA) with the KBr pellet method. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by PHI 500 Versa-Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (UlVAC-PHI, Japan). A D8 Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement at room temperature. Raman spectra were acquired by laser Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) obtained under a 325 nm laser. The FL spectra were acquired by LS SS Perkin Elmer spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) with the slit widths of 4 nm for emission and excitation spectra. And the fluorescence spectra were measured from 330 nm to 650 nm using a 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cell.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared Ag@PCPDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum, respectively. The image of TEM (Fig. 1 a) indicates that Ag@PCPDs is typical spherical and monodisperse with an average size of 7.81 ± 0.31 nm (Fig. 1b). And HR-TEM image of individual Ag@PCPDs nanoparticle displayed a typical lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was in keeping with the (100) lattice plane spacing of graphene [20]. Fig. S1 shows XRD pattern of Ag@PCPDs with a broad peak located at 23°, corresponding to the graphene-like structure of Ag@PCPDs [21]. From the results of Raman spectrum for the Ag@PCPDs (Fig. S2), it can be found two broad peaks at 1583 and 1357 cm−1, ascribed to the G (sp2 hybrid carbon) and D (sp3 hybrid carbon) bands, respectively [16]. And the intensity ratio (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.94, indicating the intrinsic defects and amorphous surface of the Ag@PCPDs [21]. The functional groups on the surface of the as-fabricated Ag@PCPDs were confirmed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1c, the characteristic peak at 3440 cm−1 belongs to the absorption band of O–H bond, and the peaks at 1427 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = C/C = O. The absorption peaks at 1181, 992, 887 and 614 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of P-O (phosphate ester group), P-O-C, P-O–H and the deformation vibration of PO4 3-, respectively [22]. The absorption at 1641 cm−1 could be ascribed to the interaction between functional groups and Ag+ through simple electrostatic attraction or the formation of coordination interaction [23].

More details on surface composition and chemical states of Ag@PCPDs could be found in XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d). The characteristic peaks emerged at 532.14, 284.9, 134.1 and 372.5 eV in full range XPS spectrum demonstrated the appearance of four elements including O, C, P and Ag with the relative atomic ratios of approximately 42.6%, 50.5%, 4.6% and 2.3%, respectively [24]. In the high resolution analysis, the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 a) can be divided into three peaks (for C–C at 284.9 eV, for C-O at 283.7 eV, and for C = O at 287.2 eV), and the O 1 s spectrum can be fitted as follows: 531.6 eV (C-O/C = O), 530.9 eV (P-O/P = O), and 532.3 eV (P-O-Ag) (Fig. 2b). The P 2p spectrum contains two peaks at 134.6 eV and 133.5 eV, which can be assigned as P-OH/P-O-C and P-O (Fig. 2c). The Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 2d) appears two peaks originating from Ag 3d5/2 (368.1 eV) and Ag 3d3/2 (374.5 eV) [25].

Generally, Ag+ can be stabilized on the surface of PCPDs through the interaction between Ag+ and oxygen-containing functional groups [22]. Upon addition of GSH into the Ag@PCPDs solution, the oxidation–reduction reaction would occur on the surface of Ag@PCPDs and Ag+ was reduced to metal Ag0, which induced in the excited-state intramolecular electron transfer and the radiative emission of PCPDs [28]. And the formation of Ag nanoclusters can make the PCPDs keep from the electronic screening of Ag+, resulting in PL increasing. To further evaluate the detection capability of the Ag@PCPDs towards GSH, the PL emission spectra of the proposed optical systems with different concentrations of GSH were tested under the optimal conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, the intensity of the maximum PL emission peak at 456 nm was increased as the addition of GSH solution whereas no significant changes were found for the location of Em . Furthermore, a significant linearity relationship between PL intensity and the concentration of GSH solution in a range of 0–200 μM was also revealed, which can be described by the equation of P/P 0 = 0.018C GSH + 1.08353 (P 0 and P are the PL intensities in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. C GSH is the concentration of GSH). An outstanding linearity (R 2 = 0.9919) of the calibration curve (the inset of Fig. 4a) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 680 nM (based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 in blank samples) was obtained, which is comparable with previously reported bio-sensors for GSH detection (Table S1). Moreover, the PL responses of the prepared sensors towards the representative interferent (AA, DA, UA, Dopac, Glu, Cys, CA, and different metal ions including Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Zn2+) were studied with the same condition. As shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S3b, the mentioned biological molecules displayed negligible effect on the PL behavior of the Ag@PCPDs except GSH, proving a superior selectivity of this sensing platform based on Ag@PCPDs for GSH detection.

In addition, a PL switching behavior of Ag@PCPDs was found with the existence of GSH and Fe3+ (Fig. S4). GSH has a positive effect on the enhancement of the PL intensity towards Ag@PCPDs. Once Fe3+ was added to Ag@PCPDs in the present of GSH, the redox reaction between GSH and Fe3+ happened prior to the PL response of Ag@PCPDs towards GSH resulting in the returning to its original PL intensity of Ag@PCPDs [29]. Based on the above PL phenomenon of Ag@PCPDs, it can be employed as molecular logic gates to demonstrate the potential for multiplexed detection of analytes [30]. In recent years, the construction of molecular logic gates based on optical signals generated by the interaction between anions, cations and molecules have become one of the research focuses of molecular logic gate [31]. In this work, GSH and Fe3+ were regarded as inputs and the PL intensity of Ag@PCPDs at 456 nm as output to create a multiple-input logic gate. For input, the absence of GSH or Fe3+ was considered as “0″ and their presence was considered as “1”. For output, the highest PL intensity was signed as “1”, and the original PL intensity was defined as “0”. As shown in Fig. 5 , when there was no analytes input or only the existence of Fe3+, Ag@PCPDs shown the original PL intensity at 456 nm giving an output signal of “0”. Only in the presence of GSH and the absence of Fe3+, the highest PL intensity was obtained, resulting in an output of 1. When GSH and Fe3+ existed together, the PL of Ag@PCPDs maintained original intensity, and the output signal was “0”. To study the selectivity of the logic gate based on Ag@PCPDs, Cys was introduced as the interferent, and the result was displayed in Fig. S5. Above results indicated that the Ag@PCPDs-based logic system can be used in next-generation logic-gate-based specific molecules recognition.

To explore the utility of this approach, the Ag@PCPDs were used for GSH determination in real samples, which were diluted with 100-fold. According to standard addition method, different concentrations of GSH solutions were introduced into the human serum and urine and tested by fluorescence spectra. From the results depicted in Table S2, a satisfactory recovery ranged from 92.81% to 107.45% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) under 4.51% was obtained. The testing results were confirmed by HPLC-MS, demonstrating the feasibility and reliability of Ag@PCPDs for detecting GSH in the presence of complex biological samples.

To further investigate the practical applications of the sensing platform, the cytotoxicity assays and cellular imaging performance experiment were carried out. As is well known, it's necessary for the prepared Ag@PCPDs to evaluate the cytotoxicity [11]. The viability of Hela cells was tested by MTT method after they were incubated with 0–500 μg mL−1 of the Ag@PCPDs for 24 h. A satisfactory cell viability maintaining above 85% was obtained in Fig. 6 , indicating the low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility of Ag@PCPDs. To further illustrate the cellular imaging performance of Ag@PCPDs, the HeLa cells were stained with Ag@PCPDs for 12 h. As shown in Fig. 7 , noticeable blue PL emission from the intracellular region was observed by fluorescence microscope. Moreover, no obvious PL intensity changed and no considerable morphology alteration in the stained HeLa cells was found during 10 min (Fig. S6), suggesting excellent cellular imaging capacity and photostability of the as-prepared Ag@PCPDs.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized silver-phosphorus co-doped CPDs (Ag@PCPDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and phytic acid in the presence of Ag+. The resultants Ag@PCPDs have excellent photostability, storage stability and pH, and various ionic strength tolerances with high PL QYs of 15.6%. The obtained Ag@PCPDs displayed typical spherical with an average size of 7.81 ± 0.31 nm, showing distinctly excitation-dependent PL behaviors. The doped structural and the functionalized surface with multiple heteroatoms were confirmed by various characterization techniques, including Raman, FT-IR, XPS, and XRD. Furthermore, the Ag@PCPDs, as a PL sensing platform, showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards GSH with turn-on PL emission. And the LOD of the proposed system for GSH was 680 nM in the linear detection range of 0–200 μM, which is comparable with previously reported bio-sensors for GSH detection. Subsequently, the developed sensors have been successfully utilized in molecular logic gate construction. Furthermore, the as-fabricated Ag@PCPDs were found to be suitable for monitoring GSH levels in real samples. Notably, the cytotoxicity assays and cellular imaging in HeLa cells were successfully carried out, suggesting that the aptasensor Ag@PCPDs could be used in biomedical applications. Thus, we consider the Ag@PCPDs have opportunities as a practical platform for cell imaging and environmental monitoring.

Xi Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yun Hu: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Lina Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yufei Li: Conceptualization. Lifang Zhang: Data curation. Yufeng Cao: Visualization, Resources. Jinqiu Zhou: Project administration, Data curation. Tao Qian: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001284, 318907733).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123227.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Journal of the American Chemical Society

Chemical Society Reviews

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red-emission carbon dots as fluorescent “on–off–on” probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and glutathione
Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turn-on fluorescence sensor for glutathione in aqueous solutions using carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites
Sens. Actuat. B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification and C-13-tracer analysis of total reduced glutathione by HPLC-QTOFMS/MS
Analytica Chimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.N. Sun, P. Heinrich, R.S. Berger, P.J. Oefner, K. Dettmer, Quantification and C-13-tracer analysis of total reduced glutathione by HPLC-QTOFMS/MS, Anal. Chim. Acta 1080 (2019) 127–137.

Biotechnology Letters

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct electrochemistry and electrocatalysis of reduced glutathione on CNFs-PDDA/PB nanocomposite film modified ITO electrode for biosensors
Colloids and Surfaces B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bovine serum albumin-encapsulated gold nanoclusters-Cu2+ synergize and promote calcein chemiluminescence for glutathione detection in human whole blood
Microchemical Journal

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent probe based on FRET effect between carbon nanodots and gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of thiourea
Spectrochimica Acta A

Food Chemistry

Fang, The facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for developing a powerful “on-off-on” fluorescence probe to detect glutathione in vegetables, Food Chem. 372 (2022) 131142–131148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and cost-effective preparation of carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in living cells based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle
Applied Surface Science

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation-dependent multiple luminescence emission of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for cysteine sensing, bioimaging, and photoluminescent ink applications
